title: Front propagation in non-homogeneous $φ^4$ model
authors: Jacek Gatlik, Tomasz Dobrowolski, Dominika Lasa, Panayotis G. Kevrekidis
abstract: We investigate the propagation of fronts in an inhomogeneous medium within the framework of the $φ^4$ model. The inhomogeneity is modeled either as an interface separating regions with different dissipation or as a finite layer with modified dissipation. The propagating front is described in two ways: as a kink solution in an effectively unbounded domain, and as a half-kink in a finite system. The half-kink represents the decay of the unstable state $φ=0$ toward the true vacuum. We show that while the effective description based on the kink provides accurate results, applying a similar approach to the half-kink leads to significant deviations from the predictions of the field model. We then demonstrate that a consistent description does exist and propose a modified effective model which reproduces the field-theory results over a relatively broad range of parameters.
summary: This work analyzes how topological fronts (kinks) propagate in media where physical parameters like dissipation change spatially. The authors develop and validate a sophisticated, modified effective model that successfully captures the complex velocity dynamics of the front, demonstrating a powerful method for simplifying complex field theories.
main_problem: The paper investigates the complex dynamics of front propagation (kinks and half-kinks) in a non-homogeneous medium governed by the $\phi^4$ model.
main_result: The authors successfully derived and proposed a modified effective model that accurately reproduces the field-theory results for the half-kink's velocity, even when passing through regions of varying dissipation.
method: The study employs a combination of deriving effective Lagrangians from the full field equation and comparing the resulting equations of motion against numerical simulations of the PDE.
model_or_system: The system is the classical $\phi^4$ model, described by a nonlinear partial differential equation that includes spatially varying dissipation $\eta(x)$ and potentially external forcing $\Gamma$.
key_observables: Kink/half-kink velocity ($v_0$), the difference between PDE and ODE solutions ($\Delta x_0(t)$), and the effective function $g(x_0)$ describing the dynamics.
important_parameters: Dissipation coefficients ($\eta_0, \epsilon$), external forcing ($\Gamma$), and system size ($\ell$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on reducing the field dynamics to a few collective coordinates (kink position and width) and proposing specific ansatzes for the effective Lagrangian.
figures_summary: Figures compare kink trajectories and velocity changes between the full PDE model and various effective ODE models (1-DOF, 2-DOF, and modified), showing good quantitative agreement for the latter.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from analyzing the driven kink in unbounded domains, to developing effective models for the half-kink in finite systems, and finally proposing and validating a modified, single-degree-of-freedom effective model that matches field theory results.
why_interesting: The techniques used for deriving effective, low-dimensional descriptions of complex, dissipative, nonlinear wave phenomena are highly relevant to modeling open quantum systems or non-equilibrium dynamics in condensed matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (a) Comparison of kink trajectories from the field equation (solid black line) and the effective model (orange dashed line). The gray area represents the time during which the kink moves in the area of changed dissipation. Panel (b) shows the difference ∆x0(t). Parameters are chosen as follows: x0 = −50, η0 = 0.1, Γ = 0.025, λ = 1, and ε = 0.05.

Figure 2: Changes in kink velocity when entering an area of increased dissipation. In both panels, the initial position of the kink is x0 = −50. Parameters in the figures are chosen as follows η0 = 0.1, Γ = 0.025, λ = 1, (a) ε = 0.05, (b) ε = 0.1.

Figure 3 shows changes in the velocity of the kink as it passes through a layer with increased dissipation and thickness L0 = 20. The parameters on both panels were selected so that the initial speeds of the kink were significantly different, i.e., (a) η0 = 0.1, Γ = 0.025 and (b) η0 = 0.04, Γ = 0.02. As before, speed deviations from the effective model appear at the beginning. Depending on the initial speed, the kink velocity decreases markedly in the layer of increased dissipation. After damping the initial oscillations, the agreement between the effective model and the field model is fairly striking. It can be seen that on both panels, the change in dissipation is at the level of one...

Figure 3: Changes in kink velocity when passing through a layer with increased dissipation. In the left panel, the initial position of the kink is x0 = −5, while in the right panel it is x0 = −100. The parameters on the panels are as follows (a) η0 = 0.1, Γ = 0.025, ε = 0.1 (b) η0 = 0.04, Γ = 0.02, ε = 0.04. On both panels L0 = 20 and λ = 1.

Figure 4: (a) Difference between the gradients of the stationary configuration and the initial condition (after shift- ing them to the same position) in case of constant dissipation η0 = 0.1, ε = 0 and Γ = 0.025. (b) Comparison of the kink velocity from field model (black line) and effective model (orange dashed line). The initial conditions in the field model were chosen based on the stationary solution. The parameters in this panel are identical to those in Fig. 3 (a) i.e., η0 = 0.1, ε = 0.1, Γ = 0.025 and x0 = −50.
